---
author: "Jacopo Olivieri"
date: 2024-08-29
editor: source
format: html
number-sections: true
number-depth: 4
execute: 
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: auto
  fig-width: 9
  fig-asp: 0.618 
  fig-align: center
---

# Dry Spill Exploration {#sec-04-dry-spills}

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# Initialize packages with version control
if (!requireNamespace("renv", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("renv")
}

# Run manual renv commands if needed
# renv::activate() # Activate the project library
# renv::restore()  # Restore the environment if running for first time
# renv::snapshot()  # After adding/updating packages, snapshot the state

# Load required packages
required_packages <- c(
  "rmarkdown",
  "rio",
  "tidyverse",
  "purrr",
  "here",
  "janitor",
  "data.table",
  "fixest",
  "sf",
  "sp",
  "rnaturalearth",
  "rnaturalearthdata",
  "rnrfa",
  "DescTools",
  "gridExtra",
  "modelsummary",
  "tmap",
  "leaflet",
  "htmltools",
  "servr",
  "viridis",
  "readxl",
  "reactable",
  "plotly",
  "ggExtra"
)
install_if_missing <- function(packages) {
  new_packages <- packages[!sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)]
  if (length(new_packages) > 0) {
    install.packages(new_packages)
  }
  invisible(sapply(packages, library, character.only = TRUE))
}
install_if_missing(required_packages)


# Initialize logging
log_path <- here::here("output", "log", "spill_data_exploration.log")
if (!dir.exists(dirname(log_path))) {
  dir.create(dirname(log_path), recursive = TRUE)
}
logger::log_appender(logger::appender_file(log_path))
logger::log_layout(logger::layout_glue_colors)

# Ggplot theme
theme_pref <- theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(size = 12),
    plot.title = element_text(
      face = "bold",
      size = 12,
      margin = ggplot2::margin(b = 10, unit = "pt")
    ),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    axis.text = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1, size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "gray95"),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "gray95"),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 10, r = 10, b = 10, l = 10, unit = "pt")
  )
```

```{css css-settings, echo = FALSE}
/* Custom CSS for modelsummary tables */
.table, th, td {
  font-size: 0.9em;  /* Base font size (matches reactable's cell style) */
}

/* Headers */
.table th {
  font-size: 0.95em !important;     /* Header font size */
  white-space: normal !important;  /* Allow header text wrapping */
  padding: 0.25em 0.5em !important;  /* Compact padding */
}

/* Cells */
.table td {
  white-space: nowrap !important;  /* Prevent cell text wrapping */
  padding: 0.25em 0.5em !important;  /* Compact padding */
}
```

```{r load-data}
# Aggregated spill data
dat_yr <- import(
  here::here(
    "data",
    "processed",
    "agg_spill_stats",
    "agg_spill_dry_yr.parquet"
  ),
)
dat_mo <- import(
  here::here(
    "data",
    "processed",
    "agg_spill_stats",
    "agg_spill_dry_mo.parquet"
  ),
)

# Rainfall data
rainfall_yr <- import(
  here::here("data", "processed", "rainfall", "rainfall_agg_yr.parquet"),
)
rainfall_mo <- import(
  here::here("data", "processed", "rainfall", "rainfall_agg_mo.parquet"),
)

# Spill sites
spill_sites <- import(
  here("data", "processed", "unique_spill_sites.parquet"),
  trust = TRUE
)

# UK Map
uk_map <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf") %>%
  filter(sovereignt %in% c("United Kingdom"))

# Load MSOA boundaries from local shapefile
msoa_boundaries <- st_read(
  here::here("data", "raw", "shapefiles", "msoa_bcg_2021"),
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  filter(str_detect(MSOA21CD, "^E")) %>% # England only
  st_simplify(dTolerance = 200) %>% # Simplify geometry for smaller file size
  select(MSOA21CD, MSOA21NM, geometry) %>%
  rename(msoa_code = MSOA21CD, msoa_name = MSOA21NM) %>%
  # Calculate area in km²
  mutate(msoa_area_km2 = as.numeric(st_area(.)) / 1000000)

# Load MSOA population data
msoa_population <- readxl::read_excel(
  here::here(
    "data",
    "raw",
    "shapefiles",
    "msoa_population_2021",
    "sapemsoasyoatablefinal.xlsx"
  ),
  sheet = "Mid-2022 MSOA 2021",
  range = "A4:E7268"
) %>%
  select(msoa_code = 3, msoa_population = 5) %>% # Column C and E
  filter(!is.na(msoa_code), !is.na(msoa_population))
```

## What Is a Dry Spill? {#sec-04-01-definition}

### Context {#sec-04-01-01-context}

Much of England's sewer network was built in the Victorian era as a **combined system**, with a single pipe carrying both wastewater from homes and businesses and runoff from rainfall. Since the 1960s, new networks have generally been built as separate systems, with sewage and surface water carried in different pipes. Even so, **around 20% of England's sewer network remains combined**.

Under normal conditions, wastewater is carried through the network to treatment works before being released into rivers or the sea. During periods of heavy rainfall, water companies are permitted to use storm overflows to discharge untreated sewage into inland waters so that the network does not back up.

![](book_figs/when_do_dry_spills_occur.png){width=60%}

A *dry spill* is the discharge of sewage in dry conditions, and it is prohibited under environmental law. Because there is no rainfall to dilute the sewage, dry spills pose a particularly high risk to receiving waters and ecosystems.

### Definitions {#sec-04-01-02-definitions}

#### Environmental Agency

The Environment Agency (EA) defines a dry spill as the use of a storm overflow on a *dry day*, meaning **no rainfall above 0.25 mm on that day or during the previous 24 hours**.^[https://environmentagency.blog.gov.uk/2024/08/28/what-are-dry-day-spills/]

Storm overflows are not expected to operate on dry days, although exceptions can occur. In large catchments, for example, rainfall upstream may take significant time to travel downstream before reaching the overflow. For this reason, the EA treats any recorded dry-day spill as a potential breach until further investigation confirms the cause.

#### BBC

In its reporting, the BBC defines a dry day more conservatively: no measurable rainfall (≥0.25 mm) during the preceding four days within a 9 $km^2$ area around each overflow site.^[https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-66670132] This approach was intended to ensure that spills flagged as occurring on dry days could not plausibly be attributed to recent rainfall still moving through the catchment. The 9 $km^2$ buffer was used as a proxy for the catchment feeding each overflow.

![BBC Methodology](book_figs/bbc_dry_spill_method.png){width=60%}

## Estimating Dry Spills {#sec-04-02-estimating-dry-spills}

```{r dry-spills-table}
# Helpers to streamline EA/BBC table creation

# 1) Published benchmarks (join by year for safety)
published_benchmarks <- tibble::tribble(
  ~year,
  ~EA,
  ~BBC,
  2021L,
  NA_real_,
  NA_real_,
  2022L,
  20000,
  6000,
  2023L,
  18000,
  NA_real_
)

# 2) Method labels (HTML)
method_labels <- c(
  dry_spill_count_yr_r0_d01_strict = "1km² Rainfall,<br>2-Day Window,<br>Complete Data",
  dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak = "9km² Rainfall,<br>2-Day Window,<br>Partial Data OK",
  dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_strict = "9km² Rainfall,<br>2-Day Window,<br>Complete Data",
  dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d0123_strict = "9km² Rainfall,<br>4-Day Window,<br>Complete Data",
  dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d0123_weak = "9km² Rainfall,<br>4-Day Window,<br>Partial Data OK"
)

method_cols <- names(method_labels)

# 3) Compute dry-spill summaries with optional BBC filters
compute_dry_summary <- function(data, sample = c("EA", "BBC"), thr = 1.25) {
  sample <- match.arg(sample)
  data_filt <- dplyr::as_tibble(data)

  if (sample == "BBC") {
    data_filt <- data_filt %>%
      dplyr::filter(
        water_company != "Welsh Water",
        !(water_company == "United Utilities" &
          spill_count_yr / spill_count_ea > thr)
      )
  }

  out <- data_filt %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      dplyr::across(starts_with("dry_spill_count"), ~ sum(., na.rm = TRUE)),
      .by = c("year")
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(c("year", method_cols))) %>%
    dplyr::left_join(published_benchmarks, by = "year")

  out
}

# 4) Build a reactable with consistent options and one highlighted method
build_dry_reactable <- function(
  data,
  published = c("EA", "BBC"),
  highlight_method
) {
  published <- match.arg(published)
  # Keep only desired columns in the rendered table
  data <- dplyr::select(data, dplyr::all_of(c("year", method_cols, published)))

  # Base colDefs for methods
  method_defs <- lapply(method_cols, function(col) {
    colDef(
      name = method_labels[[col]],
      html = TRUE,
      format = colFormat(separators = TRUE),
      align = "center",
      cell = function(value) if (is.na(value)) "N/A" else value,
      style = if (identical(col, highlight_method)) {
        list(backgroundColor = "#e8f5e8")
      } else {
        NULL
      }
    )
  })
  names(method_defs) <- method_cols

  # Published column def
  published_def <- setNames(
    list(colDef(
      name = paste0(published, "<br>Estimate"),
      html = TRUE,
      align = "center",
      format = colFormat(separators = TRUE),
      cell = function(value) if (is.na(value)) "N/A" else value,
      style = list(backgroundColor = "#fff3e0")
    )),
    published
  )

  reactable(
    data,
    columns = c(
      list(
        year = colDef(name = "Year", align = "center", width = 80)
      ),
      method_defs,
      published_def
    ),
    columnGroups = list(
      colGroup(
        name = "Our Analysis Methods",
        columns = method_cols
      ),
      colGroup(
        name = "Published Estimates",
        columns = c(published)
      )
    ),
    defaultSorted = "year",
    defaultSortOrder = "asc",
    filterable = FALSE,
    resizable = TRUE,
    compact = TRUE,
    defaultPageSize = 10,
    showPageSizeOptions = FALSE,
    showPagination = FALSE
  )
}

# 5) Compose title and notes (shared)
table_title <- tags$div(
  tags$h4(
    "Yearly Dry Spill Counts Under Alternative Methods",
    style = "margin-bottom: 5px;"
  ),
  tags$p(
    "Comparison of dry-spill estimation methods vs. published EA/BBC benchmarks",
    style = "font-size: 0.9em; color: #555; margin-top: 0;"
  )
)

table_notes <- tags$div(
  style = "font-size: 0.9em; color: #666; text-align: left; margin: 5px 0 10px 0;",
  tags$strong("Methodology Notes:"),
  tags$ul(
    style = "margin: 5px 0; padding-left: 20px;",
    tags$li(HTML(
      "<strong>Rainfall Coverage:</strong> <em>1km²</em> uses the nearest grid; <em>9km²</em> uses a 3×3 grid around each site (BBC)."
    )),
    tags$li(HTML(
      "<strong>Temporal Window:</strong> <em>2-day</em> (day 0–1) vs <em>4-day</em> (day 0–3) lookback."
    )),
    tags$li(HTML(
      "<strong>Data Handling:</strong> <em>Complete</em> requires full rainfall coverage; <em>Partial OK</em> allows missing values when applying the 0.25mm threshold."
    )),
    tags$li(HTML(
      "<strong>Highlighting:</strong> <span style='background-color: #e8f5e8; padding: 2px 4px;'>Light green</span> marks the preferred method for each sample; <span style='background-color: #fff3e0; padding: 2px 4px;'>Light orange</span> marks the published benchmark."
    ))
  )
)

# 6) Build summaries and tables
thr <- 1.25 # BBC data exclusion threshold for United Utilities consistency check
united22 <- dat_yr %>%
  filter(water_company == "United Utilities") %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    prop_gt_1.3 = mean(spill_count_yr / spill_count_ea > thr, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  pull(prop_gt_1.3)

dry_spills_summary_ea <- compute_dry_summary(dat_yr, sample = "EA", thr = thr)
dry_spills_summary_bbc <- compute_dry_summary(dat_yr, sample = "BBC", thr = thr)

reactable_table_ea <- build_dry_reactable(
  dry_spills_summary_ea,
  published = "EA",
  highlight_method = "dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak" # 9km², 2-day, partial OK
)

reactable_table_bbc <- build_dry_reactable(
  dry_spills_summary_bbc,
  published = "BBC",
  highlight_method = "dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d0123_weak" # 9km², 4-day, partial OK (BBC)
)

# 7) Display: EA and BBC tables with shared title & notes
final_output_ea <- tags$div(table_title, reactable_table_ea, table_notes)
final_output_bbc <- tags$div(table_title, reactable_table_bbc, table_notes)
```

We estimate dry spills using several alternative methods and compare them with the figures reported by the EA^[https://environmentagency.blog.gov.uk/2024/10/10/regulating-the-water-industry-our-approach-what-were-changing-and-new-powers-in-the-water-bill/] and the BBC^[https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c4nn46rjej6o].

We present two versions of the dry-spill totals to reflect differences in published samples:

- **EA sample**: aggregates across all companies and sites.
- **BBC sample**: excludes Welsh Water and removes the subset of United Utilities sites whose discharge data appear inconsistent with the 2022 annual report summary.

The EA reports **20,000 dry spills in 2022** and **18,000 in 2023**. Our closest estimates use the criterion of no measurable rainfall (≥0.25 mm) in the preceding two days within either a 1 $km^2$ grid cell (column 1) or a 9 $km^2$ buffer (column 2). We prefer the latter because it is slightly more conservative.

```{r ea}
final_output_ea
```

The BBC uses a more conservative methodology and reports **6,000 dry spills in 2022**. To create a comparable sample, we exclude Welsh Water and remove any United Utilities overflow site where the annual spill count exceeds 25% of the EA's published total for that site.^[https://environment.data.gov.uk/dataset/21e15f12-0df8-4bfc-b763-45226c16a8ac] (see also @sec-02-02-incorrect-spill_data). This adjustment removes 36% of United Utilities' sites, broadly matching the BBC's approach.

Our closest estimates apply the criterion of no measurable rainfall (≥0.25 mm) in the preceding four days within a 9 $km^2$ buffer around each spill site with complete rainfall coverage (column 4). We prefer the version that keeps sites with missing rainfall data (column 5), since the dry-spill site map in @sec-04-03-dry-spill-locations shows that missing data is concentrated almost entirely along the coast.

```{r bbc}
final_output_bbc
```

From this point on, we report results using two definitions only: **(i) no measurable rainfall (≥0.25 mm) in the preceding two days within a 9 $km^2$ buffer, and (ii) no measurable rainfall in the preceding four days within a 9 $km^2$ buffer.**

## Daily Rainfall and Spill Probability {#sec-04-10-daily-rain-spill}

At the daily site level, `spill_count` is binary (0/1), so the rainfall regressions focus on spill incidence (`spill_any`). We estimate distributed-lag logit models for the probability of a spill day and then use spill hours to show how intensity varies across rainfall regimes.

```{r prepare-daily-panel}
daily_path <- here::here(
  "data",
  "processed",
  "agg_spill_stats",
  "agg_spill_daily.parquet"
)

dat_daily <- arrow::read_parquet(
  daily_path,
  col_select = c(
    "site_id",
    "water_company",
    "date",
    "year",
    "spill_count",
    "spill_hrs",
    "rainfall_max_9cell_d0_na_rm",
    "site_missing"
  )
) |>
  data.table::as.data.table()

dat_daily <- dat_daily[
  site_missing == FALSE &
    !is.na(spill_count) &
    !is.na(rainfall_max_9cell_d0_na_rm)
]

dat_daily[, spill_any := as.integer(spill_count == 1L)]
data.table::setorder(dat_daily, site_id, date)

dat_daily[, `:=`(
  rain_t = rainfall_max_9cell_d0_na_rm,
  rain_l1 = data.table::shift(rainfall_max_9cell_d0_na_rm, 1L),
  rain_l2 = data.table::shift(rainfall_max_9cell_d0_na_rm, 2L),
  rain_l3 = data.table::shift(rainfall_max_9cell_d0_na_rm, 3L),
  rain_l4 = data.table::shift(rainfall_max_9cell_d0_na_rm, 4L),
  rain_l5 = data.table::shift(rainfall_max_9cell_d0_na_rm, 5L),
  rain_l6 = data.table::shift(rainfall_max_9cell_d0_na_rm, 6L),
  rain_l7 = data.table::shift(rainfall_max_9cell_d0_na_rm, 7L)
), by = site_id]

dat_daily[, `:=`(
  dry_t = as.integer(rain_t < 0.25),
  dry_l1 = as.integer(rain_l1 < 0.25),
  dry_l2 = as.integer(rain_l2 < 0.25),
  dry_l3 = as.integer(rain_l3 < 0.25),
  dry_l4 = as.integer(rain_l4 < 0.25),
  dry_l5 = as.integer(rain_l5 < 0.25),
  dry_l6 = as.integer(rain_l6 < 0.25),
  dry_l7 = as.integer(rain_l7 < 0.25)
)]

dat_daily_lag4 <- dat_daily[!is.na(rain_l4)]
dat_daily_lag7 <- dat_daily[!is.na(rain_l7)]

binary_check <- dat_daily[, .N, by = spill_count][order(spill_count)]
knitr::kable(binary_check, caption = "Daily spill_count is binary in the estimation sample")
```

### Distributed-Lag Regressions (Logit) {#sec-04-10-01-distributed-lag}

To quantify how rainfall at different horizons predicts spill incidence, we estimate distributed-lag logit models where the dependent variable is a binary indicator for whether a spill occurred on a given site-day. We include contemporaneous rainfall and up to four or seven daily lags, clustering standard errors at the site level to account for serial correlation within overflow sites.

```{r daily-distributed-lag-models}
mod_daily_lag4 <- fixest::feglm(
  spill_any ~ rain_t + rain_l1 + rain_l2 + rain_l3 + rain_l4,
  data = dat_daily_lag4,
  family = "binomial",
  vcov = ~site_id
)

mod_daily_lag7 <- fixest::feglm(
  spill_any ~ rain_t + rain_l1 + rain_l2 + rain_l3 + rain_l4 + rain_l5 + rain_l6 + rain_l7,
  data = dat_daily_lag7,
  family = "binomial",
  vcov = ~site_id
)

coef_map_rain <- c(
  rain_t = "Rain (t)",
  rain_l1 = "Rain (t-1)",
  rain_l2 = "Rain (t-2)",
  rain_l3 = "Rain (t-3)",
  rain_l4 = "Rain (t-4)",
  rain_l5 = "Rain (t-5)",
  rain_l6 = "Rain (t-6)",
  rain_l7 = "Rain (t-7)"
)

modelsummary::modelsummary(
  list(
    "Logit: Lags 0-4" = mod_daily_lag4,
    "Logit: Lags 0-7" = mod_daily_lag7
  ),
  coef_map = coef_map_rain,
  estimate = "{estimate}{stars}",
  statistic = "({std.error})",
  gof_omit = "AIC|BIC|Log.Lik|F|RMSE|Pseudo|Within|R2",
  stars = c("*" = 0.1, "**" = 0.05, "***" = 0.01),
  output = "markdown"
)
```

All coefficients are highly significant and positive, confirming that rainfall raises the probability of a spill. The effect is heavily front-loaded: contemporaneous rainfall has the largest coefficient (≈0.099 in log-odds), with the first lag nearly as large (≈0.092). By the second day the coefficient drops by roughly two-thirds, and it continues to decay gradually through lags 3–7. Even at lag 7, the effect remains statistically significant (≈0.009), suggesting that antecedent moisture conditions matter for at least a week—consistent with the time required for stormwater to percolate through catchments and reach the sewer network.

The 7-lag specification yields only a modestly higher pseudo-$R^2$ (0.174 vs. 0.172), indicating that the first five days capture most of the predictive content. This supports the short look-back windows used by the EA and the BBC (see @sec-04-01-02-definitions).

#### Distributed-Lag Coefficient Plot {#sec-04-10-01-plot}

```{r daily-distributed-lag-coef-plot}
extract_lag_plot_data <- function(model, model_label, prefix = "rain_") {
  coef_vec <- stats::coef(model)
  vcov_mat <- stats::vcov(model)
  se_vec <- sqrt(diag(vcov_mat))

  out <- data.table::data.table(
    term = names(coef_vec),
    estimate = as.numeric(coef_vec),
    std_error = as.numeric(se_vec[names(coef_vec)]),
    model = model_label
  )

  out <- out[grepl(paste0("^", prefix), term)]

  prefix_base <- sub("_$", "", prefix)
  term_t <- paste0(prefix_base, "_t")
  term_l <- paste0(prefix_base, "_l")

  out[, lag := ifelse(term == term_t, 0L, as.integer(sub(term_l, "", term)))]
  out[, `:=`(
    ci_low = estimate - 1.96 * std_error,
    ci_high = estimate + 1.96 * std_error
  )]
  out[order(lag)]
}

lag_plot_data <- data.table::rbindlist(
  list(
    extract_lag_plot_data(mod_daily_lag4, "Lags 0-4"),
    extract_lag_plot_data(mod_daily_lag7, "Lags 0-7")
  ),
  use.names = TRUE,
  fill = TRUE
)

ggplot(
  lag_plot_data,
  aes(x = lag, y = estimate, color = model, group = model)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    width = 0.15,
    alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:7) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "magma", end = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Distributed-Lag Rainfall Effects on Spill Probability (Logit Coefficients)",
    x = "Lag (days)",
    y = "Coefficient on rainfall (log-odds scale)",
    color = "Specification"
  ) +
  theme_pref
```

The coefficient plot makes the decay structure visually apparent: the effect of rainfall on spill probability is concentrated in the first two days, with a sharp drop-off between lag 1 and lag 2. The two specifications overlap closely for lags 0–4, while the additional lags 5–7 remain positive but small, with confidence intervals narrowing toward zero.

### 0.25 mm Threshold Models {#sec-04-10-02-threshold}

The continuous-rainfall specifications above show a clear positive relationship between rainfall and spills. A related question is whether the dry-day rule itself, defined using the 0.25 mm threshold in @sec-04-01-02-definitions, predicts lower spill probability. That is the rule that underpins our dry-spill counts. We therefore replace the continuous rainfall lags with dry-day indicators ($\mathbb{1}[\text{rain} < 0.25\text{mm}]$) at each lag.

```{r daily-threshold-models}
mod_threshold_2day <- fixest::feglm(
  spill_any ~ dry_t + dry_l1,
  data = dat_daily[!is.na(dry_l1)],
  family = "binomial",
  vcov = ~site_id
)

mod_threshold_7day <- fixest::feglm(
  spill_any ~ dry_t + dry_l1 + dry_l2 + dry_l3 + dry_l4 + dry_l5 + dry_l6 + dry_l7,
  data = dat_daily_lag7,
  family = "binomial",
  vcov = ~site_id
)

coef_map_dry <- c(
  dry_t = "Dry day (t < 0.25mm)",
  dry_l1 = "Dry day (t-1)",
  dry_l2 = "Dry day (t-2)",
  dry_l3 = "Dry day (t-3)",
  dry_l4 = "Dry day (t-4)",
  dry_l5 = "Dry day (t-5)",
  dry_l6 = "Dry day (t-6)",
  dry_l7 = "Dry day (t-7)"
)

modelsummary::modelsummary(
  list(
    "Threshold: 2-day (t,t-1)" = mod_threshold_2day,
    "Threshold: 8-day (t...t-7)" = mod_threshold_7day
  ),
  coef_map = coef_map_dry,
  estimate = "{estimate}{stars}",
  statistic = "({std.error})",
  gof_omit = "AIC|BIC|Log.Lik|F|RMSE|Pseudo|Within|R2",
  stars = c("*" = 0.1, "**" = 0.05, "***" = 0.01),
  output = "markdown"
)
```

All dry-day coefficients are large, negative, and highly significant, confirming that dry conditions substantially reduce the odds of a spill. Contemporaneous dryness has the strongest effect (≈−1.17 in log-odds in the 8-day specification), followed closely by the first lag (≈−0.99). The magnitude drops sharply at lag 2 (≈−0.36) and stabilises around −0.11 to −0.15 for lags 3–7. This mirrors the continuous-rainfall results: the first two days carry the most information, with diminishing but non-negligible effects persisting through the week.

The two-day specification—using only $t$ and $t{-}1$—captures the dominant share of the dry-day signal, providing empirical support for the EA's two-day look-back window. The four-day window used by the BBC adds further conservatism but only modest additional explanatory power.

```{r daily-threshold-coef-plot}
threshold_plot_data <- extract_lag_plot_data(
  mod_threshold_7day,
  "Dry-day threshold lags",
  prefix = "dry_"
)

threshold_plot_data[, lag_label := paste0("t-", lag)]
threshold_plot_data[lag == 0, lag_label := "t"]

ggplot(
  threshold_plot_data,
  aes(x = lag, y = estimate)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    width = 0.15,
    color = viridis::viridis(1, option = "magma", end = 0.8)
  ) +
  geom_line(color = viridis::viridis(1, option = "magma", end = 0.8), linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(color = viridis::viridis(1, option = "magma", end = 0.8), size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = 0:7,
    labels = c("t", "t-1", "t-2", "t-3", "t-4", "t-5", "t-6", "t-7")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "0.25mm Dry-Day Threshold Effects on Spill Probability",
    x = "Lag (days)",
    y = "Coefficient on dry-day indicator (log-odds scale)"
  ) +
  theme_pref
```

The coefficient plot reinforces the step-function pattern: the dry-day effect is strongest at lags 0 and 1, drops abruptly at lag 2, and flattens thereafter. This suggests that the 0.25 mm threshold effectively separates "wet" from "dry" conditions for the purpose of spill classification, and that the most informative look-back is two days. The persistence of small but significant effects at longer lags is consistent with slow drainage in some catchments, but the marginal predictive gain from extending the window beyond four days is limited.

### Spill-Hours Distribution by Rain Regime {#sec-04-10-03-distribution}

The logit models above focus on whether a spill happens. This section turns to *intensity*, measured in spill hours, across four rainfall regimes: dry day (≤0.25 mm), moderate rain (>0.25-10 mm), heavy rain (>10-20 mm), and very heavy rain (>20 mm).

```{r daily-rain-regimes}
rain_regime_levels <- c(
  "Dry day (0-0.25 mm)",
  "Moderate (0.25-10 mm)",
  "Heavy (10-20 mm)",
  "Very heavy (>20 mm)"
)

dat_daily[, rain_regime := cut(
  rain_t,
  breaks = c(-Inf, 0.25, 10, 20, Inf),
  labels = rain_regime_levels,
  right = TRUE,
  include.lowest = TRUE
)]

regime_summary <- dat_daily[
  !is.na(rain_regime),
  .(
    obs_site_days = .N,
    spill_days = sum(spill_any, na.rm = TRUE),
    share_spill_days = mean(spill_any, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_spill_hrs = mean(spill_hrs, na.rm = TRUE),
    p10_spill_hrs = as.numeric(stats::quantile(spill_hrs, probs = 0.10, na.rm = TRUE)),
    p25_spill_hrs = as.numeric(stats::quantile(spill_hrs, probs = 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)),
    p50_spill_hrs = as.numeric(stats::quantile(spill_hrs, probs = 0.50, na.rm = TRUE)),
    p75_spill_hrs = as.numeric(stats::quantile(spill_hrs, probs = 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)),
    p90_spill_hrs = as.numeric(stats::quantile(spill_hrs, probs = 0.90, na.rm = TRUE))
  ),
  by = rain_regime
]

regime_summary[, rain_regime := factor(rain_regime, levels = rain_regime_levels)]
data.table::setorder(regime_summary, rain_regime)

reactable(
  regime_summary,
  columns = list(
    rain_regime = colDef(
      name = "Daily Rainfall",
      minWidth = 150
    ),
    obs_site_days = colDef(
      name = "N",
      format = colFormat(separators = TRUE, digits = 0)
    ),
    spill_days = colDef(
      name = "# Spill Days",
      format = colFormat(separators = TRUE, digits = 0)
    ),
    share_spill_days = colDef(
      name = "Share Spill Days",
      format = colFormat(percent = TRUE, digits = 1)
    ),
    mean_spill_hrs = colDef(
      name = "Mean (hrs)",
      format = colFormat(separators = TRUE, digits = 2)
    ),
    p10_spill_hrs = colDef(
      name = "P10",
      format = colFormat(separators = TRUE, digits = 2)
    ),
    p25_spill_hrs = colDef(
      name = "P25",
      format = colFormat(separators = TRUE, digits = 2)
    ),
    p50_spill_hrs = colDef(
      name = "P50",
      format = colFormat(separators = TRUE, digits = 2)
    ),
    p75_spill_hrs = colDef(
      name = "P75",
      format = colFormat(separators = TRUE, digits = 2)
    ),
    p90_spill_hrs = colDef(
      name = "P90",
      format = colFormat(separators = TRUE, digits = 2)
    )
  ),
  columnGroups = list(
    colGroup(
      name = "Observations",
      columns = c("obs_site_days")
    ),
    colGroup(
      name = "Spill Days",
      columns = c("spill_days", "share_spill_days")
    ),
    colGroup(
      name = "Spill Hours Distribution",
      columns = c(
        "mean_spill_hrs",
        "p10_spill_hrs",
        "p25_spill_hrs",
        "p50_spill_hrs",
        "p75_spill_hrs",
        "p90_spill_hrs"
      )
    )
  ),
  defaultColDef = colDef(
    headerStyle = list(
      fontSize = "0.8em",
      whiteSpace = "wrap"
    ),
    style = list(
      fontSize = "0.75em"
    ),
    minWidth = 80
  ),
  resizable = TRUE,
  filterable = FALSE,
  defaultPageSize = 10,
  compact = TRUE,
  style = list(maxWidth = "100%", overflowX = "auto")
)
```

The gradient across regimes remains steep and monotonic under this broader classification. Dry days are associated with the lowest spill incidence and shortest events, while moderate, heavy, and very heavy rainfall days show progressively higher spill probabilities and longer spill durations. The upper tail of the spill-hours distribution also thickens as rainfall intensifies, indicating that both the likelihood and duration of spills increase materially once daily rainfall rises above the dry-day threshold.

```{r daily-ecdf-spill-hours-unconditional}
set.seed(123)
ecdf_unconditional <- dat_daily[
  !is.na(rain_regime),
  .SD[sample.int(.N, min(.N, 150000L))],
  by = rain_regime
]

ggplot(
  ecdf_unconditional,
  aes(x = spill_hrs, color = rain_regime)
) +
  stat_ecdf(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "magma", end = 0.9) +
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "log1p") +
  labs(
    title = "ECDF of Spill Hours by Rainfall Regime (All Site-Days)",
    x = "Spill hours (log1p scale)",
    y = "Cumulative share of site-days",
    color = "Daily Rainfall"
  ) +
  theme_pref
```

The empirical CDFs confirm that the dry-day distribution is stacked heavily at zero: the curve rises almost vertically near the origin for the `Dry day (0-0.25 mm)` regime, indicating that the vast majority of dry site-days record no spill at all. Wetter regimes shift the distribution rightward, with the `Very heavy (>20 mm)` curve notably flatter and reaching higher spill-hour values before converging to 1. The separation between regimes is clearest in the lower tail, suggesting that rainfall primarily affects whether a spill occurs rather than its duration conditional on occurrence.

```{r daily-spill-hours-conditional-violin}
set.seed(123)
spill_pos <- dat_daily[
  spill_any == 1 & !is.na(rain_regime),
  .SD[sample.int(.N, min(.N, 100000L))],
  by = rain_regime
]

ggplot(
  spill_pos,
  aes(x = rain_regime, y = log1p(spill_hrs), fill = rain_regime)
) +
  geom_violin(trim = FALSE, alpha = 0.7, scale = "width") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.12, fill = "white", outlier.shape = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "magma", end = 0.9) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Spill Hours on Spill Days by Rainfall Regime",
    x = "Daily Rainfall",
    y = "log(1 + spill hours)"
  ) +
  theme_pref +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

The violin plots, restricted to site-days where a spill did occur, show that even conditional on spilling, wetter regimes produce longer events. The median spill duration increases with rainfall, and the upper tails of the distributions stretch progressively further. However, the overlap between regimes is substantial: short spills occur even under heavy rainfall, and long spills occasionally arise on relatively dry days. This dispersion likely reflects heterogeneity in sewer capacity, catchment size, and local drainage conditions.

Overall, the evidence lines up well with the rainfall-based dry-spill classification. Contemporaneous and one-day-lagged rainfall are the dominant predictors of spill incidence, the 0.25 mm threshold cleanly separates spill-prone from low-risk conditions, and spill intensity rises steadily across rainfall regimes. The two-day look-back window captures most of the rainfall signal, while extending to four days adds conservatism with only limited extra predictive power, consistent with the choices validated in @sec-04-02-estimating-dry-spills.

## Dry Spill vs Rainfall {#sec-04-06-dry-vs-rain}

The daily models above use day-level data. The monthly plots below show the same relationship at a coarser level and make it easier to see how dry-spill counts, hours, and dry-spill shares vary with rainfall.

```{r prepare-dry-rain}
dat_mo_rain <- left_join(
  dat_mo, rainfall_mo, 
  by = join_by(site_id, water_company, month_id)
)

# Build reusable site-month dataset with rainfall and dry spill metrics
dat_mo_rain_df <- dat_mo_rain |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dry_rate = dplyr::if_else(
      spill_count_mo > 0,
      dry_spill_count_mo_r1_d01_weak / spill_count_mo,
      NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(rainfall_r9_mo))
```

### Scatter with Marginal Distributions {#sec-04-06-01-scatter}

```{r scatter-marginals}
base_point_col <- viridis::viridis(1, option = "magma")

# Scatter: Count
p_scatter_count <- ggplot(
  dat_mo_rain_df,
  aes(x = rainfall_r9_mo, y = dry_spill_count_mo_r1_d01_weak)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.7, color = base_point_col) +
  labs(
    title = "Rainfall vs Dry Spill Count (Site-Month)",
    x = "Monthly Rainfall (mm, 9-cell)",
    y = "Dry Spill Count"
  ) +
  theme_pref

plot_scatter_count_marg <- ggExtra::ggMarginal(
  p_scatter_count,
  type = "histogram",
  fill = "grey70",
  alpha = 0.6,
  bins = 40
)

# Scatter: Duration
p_scatter_hrs <- ggplot(
  dat_mo_rain_df,
  aes(x = rainfall_r9_mo, y = dry_spill_hrs_mo_r1_d01_weak)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.7, color = base_point_col) +
  labs(
    title = "Rainfall vs Dry Spill Duration (Site-Month)",
    x = "Monthly Rainfall (mm, 9-cell)",
    y = "Dry Spill Duration (hours)"
  ) +
  theme_pref

plot_scatter_hrs_marg <- ggExtra::ggMarginal(
  p_scatter_hrs,
  type = "histogram",
  fill = "grey70",
  alpha = 0.6,
  bins = 40
)

# Scatter: Rate (dry/total)
p_scatter_rate <- ggplot(
  dplyr::filter(dat_mo_rain_df, !is.na(dry_rate) & is.finite(dry_rate)),
  aes(x = rainfall_r9_mo, y = dry_rate)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, size = 0.7, color = base_point_col) +
  labs(
    title = "Rainfall vs Share of Dry/All Spills (Site-Month)",
    x = "Monthly Rainfall (mm, 9-cell)",
    y = "Dry Spills / Total Spills"
  ) +
  theme_pref

plot_scatter_rate_marg <- ggExtra::ggMarginal(
  p_scatter_rate,
  type = "histogram",
  fill = "grey70",
  alpha = 0.6,
  bins = 40
)
```

::: panel-tabset
#### Count

```{r show-scatter-marg-count}
plot_scatter_count_marg
```

#### Duration

```{r show-scatter-marg-hrs}
plot_scatter_hrs_marg
```

#### Rate

```{r show-scatter-marg-rate}
plot_scatter_rate_marg
```

:::

## Location of Dry Spills {#sec-04-03-dry-spill-locations}

The map below shows all sites where dry spills occurred between 2021–2023, categorized by the most restrictive condition under which they experienced dry spills. Sites are colored using a traffic-light system:

- **🔴 Red**: Sites with dry spills under the strictest *4-day* window with *complete data required* (full rainfall coverage, no missing values)
- **🟠 Orange**: Sites with dry spills under the *4-day* window with *partial data allowed*
- **🟢 Green**: Sites with dry spills under the  *2-day* window with *partial data allowed* (allows missing values when applying the 0.25mm threshold)

We prefer including sites with missing rainfall data (orange), as these are concentrated along the coast, where exclusion would remove otherwise valid estimates.

```{r spill-sites-map}
#| eval: false

# Aggregate dry spill data by site across all years
spill_sites_dry <- dat_yr %>%
  dplyr::group_by(site_id, water_company) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    # Sum across years for each condition
    dry_spill_weak_total = sum(dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak, na.rm = TRUE),
    dry_spill_moderate_total = sum(
      dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d0123_weak,
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    dry_spill_strict_total = sum(
      dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d0123_strict,
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    # Keep yearly breakdown for popups
    dry_spills_2021 = sum(
      ifelse(
        year == 2021,
        pmax(
          dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak,
          dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d0123_weak,
          dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d0123_strict,
          na.rm = TRUE
        ),
        0
      ),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    dry_spills_2022 = sum(
      ifelse(
        year == 2022,
        pmax(
          dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak,
          dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d0123_weak,
          dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d0123_strict,
          na.rm = TRUE
        ),
        0
      ),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    dry_spills_2023 = sum(
      ifelse(
        year == 2023,
        pmax(
          dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak,
          dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d0123_weak,
          dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d0123_strict,
          na.rm = TRUE
        ),
        0
      ),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  # Filter to sites with at least one dry spill under any condition
  dplyr::filter(
    dry_spill_weak_total > 0 |
      dry_spill_moderate_total > 0 |
      dry_spill_strict_total > 0
  ) %>%
  # Create hierarchical categorization (strict hierarchy)
  dplyr::mutate(
    condition_category = dplyr::case_when(
      dry_spill_strict_total > 0 ~ "Strictest (4-day, complete data)",
      dry_spill_moderate_total > 0 ~ "Moderate (4-day, partial data OK)",
      dry_spill_weak_total > 0 ~ "Weakest (2-day, partial data OK)",
      TRUE ~ "No dry spills"
    ),
    # Assign colors for traffic light system
    fill_color = dplyr::case_when(
      dry_spill_strict_total > 0 ~ "#C62828", # Dark red
      dry_spill_moderate_total > 0 ~ "#FF9800", # Orange/amber
      dry_spill_weak_total > 0 ~ "#7CB342", # Light green/yellow-green
      TRUE ~ "#808080" # Gray (shouldn't appear due to filtering)
    ),
    # Total dry spills (using maximum across conditions for each year to avoid double counting)
    total_dry_spills = dry_spills_2021 + dry_spills_2022 + dry_spills_2023
  ) %>%
  # Join with spill sites for coordinates
  dplyr::left_join(spill_sites, by = c("site_id", "water_company"))

# Create function for dry spill sites map
plot_dry_spill_sites_tmap <- function(data) {
  # Convert spill sites to sf object
  sites_sf <- data %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(easting), !is.na(northing)) %>%
    st_as_sf(coords = c("easting", "northing"), crs = 27700) %>%
    st_transform(crs = 4326)

  # Create interactive map with OpenStreetMap background
  tmap_mode("view")

  # Create separate layers for each condition category to ensure proper layering
  map_base <- tm_basemap("OpenStreetMap")

  # Add points in reverse order (weakest first, then moderate, then strictest on top)
  sites_weak <- sites_sf %>%
    dplyr::filter(condition_category == "Weakest (2-day, partial data OK)")
  sites_moderate <- sites_sf %>%
    dplyr::filter(condition_category == "Moderate (4-day, partial data OK)")
  sites_strict <- sites_sf %>%
    dplyr::filter(condition_category == "Strictest (4-day, complete data)")

  final_map <- map_base

  # Layer 1: Weakest condition (light green)
  if (nrow(sites_weak) > 0) {
    final_map <- final_map +
      tm_shape(sites_weak) +
      tm_dots(
        size = 0.4,
        fill = "#7CB342",
        fill_alpha = 0.8,
        col = "white",
        lwd = 0.5,
        popup.vars = c(
          "Water Company" = "water_company",
          "Condition Category" = "condition_category",
          "Dry Spills 2021" = "dry_spills_2021",
          "Dry Spills 2022" = "dry_spills_2022",
          "Dry Spills 2023" = "dry_spills_2023",
          "Total Dry Spills" = "total_dry_spills"
        )
      )
  }

  # Layer 2: Moderate condition (orange)
  if (nrow(sites_moderate) > 0) {
    final_map <- final_map +
      tm_shape(sites_moderate) +
      tm_dots(
        size = 0.4,
        fill = "#FF9800",
        fill_alpha = 0.8,
        col = "white",
        lwd = 0.5,
        popup.vars = c(
          "Water Company" = "water_company",
          "Condition Category" = "condition_category",
          "Dry Spills 2021" = "dry_spills_2021",
          "Dry Spills 2022" = "dry_spills_2022",
          "Dry Spills 2023" = "dry_spills_2023",
          "Total Dry Spills" = "total_dry_spills"
        )
      )
  }

  # Layer 3: Strictest condition (dark red) - on top
  if (nrow(sites_strict) > 0) {
    final_map <- final_map +
      tm_shape(sites_strict) +
      tm_dots(
        size = 0.4,
        fill = "#C62828",
        fill_alpha = 0.8,
        col = "white",
        lwd = 0.5,
        popup.vars = c(
          "Water Company" = "water_company",
          "Condition Category" = "condition_category",
          "Dry Spills 2021" = "dry_spills_2021",
          "Dry Spills 2022" = "dry_spills_2022",
          "Dry Spills 2023" = "dry_spills_2023",
          "Total Dry Spills" = "total_dry_spills"
        )
      )
  }

  final_map +
    tm_view(set_view = c(-2, 53, 6))
}

# Create the map
dry_spill_sites_map <- plot_dry_spill_sites_tmap(spill_sites_dry)
```

```{r save-spill-sites-map}
#| eval: false

# Save the interactive map
path <- here("book", "book_figs", "unique_dry_spill_sites.html")
tmap_save(
  dry_spill_sites_map,
  filename = path,
  selfcontained = TRUE
)
```

```{=html}
<iframe src="book_figs/unique_dry_spill_sites.html" width="800" height="600" frameborder="0"></iframe>
```

## Dry Spill Geographic Distribution in 2023 {#sec-04-04-geo-distribution}

```{r plot-dry-spill-stats}
#| eval: false

# Join yearly dry spill aggregates with site metadata
# Why: ensure coordinates and company/site attributes are available for spatial aggregation
dat_yr_clean <- left_join(
  select(dat_yr, -ngr),
  spill_sites,
  by = join_by(water_company, site_id)
)

# Choropleth helper for consistent, readable dry spill maps
# Why: enforce consistent symbology and avoid re-implementing mapping boilerplate
plot_dry_spill_map_tmap <- function(data, value_col, legend_title) {
  data_for_map <- data %>%
    mutate(
      # Categorise to show zeros distinctly and avoid skewed colour scales
      fill_category = case_when(
        .data[[value_col]] == 0 ~ "No Dry Spills",
        .data[[value_col]] > 0 ~ "Has Dry Spills"
      ),
      # Use continuous scale only for areas with positive values
      fill_value = ifelse(.data[[value_col]] == 0, NA, .data[[value_col]])
    ) %>%
    select(
      msoa_name,
      msoa_code,
      site_count,
      water_companies,
      dry_spill_count_yr,
      dry_spill_hrs_yr,
      dry_spill_count_yr_pop_density_weighted,
      dry_spill_hrs_yr_pop_density_weighted,
      log_dry_spill_count_yr,
      log_dry_spill_hrs_yr,
      fill_category,
      fill_value,
      all_of(value_col)
    )

  tm_basemap("CartoDB.Positron") +
    # Layer 1: categorical layer to separate zeros
    tm_shape(data_for_map) +
    tm_polygons(
      fill = "fill_category",
      fill_alpha = 0.7,
      col = alpha("grey95", 0.3),
      lwd = 0.01,
      fill.scale = tm_scale(
        values = c("No Dry Spills" = "grey90", "Has Dry Spills" = "#440154")
      ),
      fill.legend = tm_legend_hide()
    ) +
    # Layer 2: continuous scale for positive values only
    tm_shape(filter(data_for_map, fill_category == "Has Dry Spills")) +
    tm_polygons(
      fill = "fill_value",
      fill_alpha = 0.7,
      col = alpha("grey95", 0.3),
      lwd = 0.01,
      fill.scale = tm_scale(values = "-magma", interval = "continuous"),
      fill.legend = tm_legend(
        title = legend_title,
        position = c("left", "bottom")
      ),
      popup.vars = c(
        "MSOA Name" = "msoa_name",
        "Number of Sites" = "site_count",
        "Water Companies" = "water_companies",
        "Total Dry Spill Count" = "dry_spill_count_yr",
        "Total Dry Spill Hours" = "dry_spill_hrs_yr",
        "Pop-Density Weighted Dry Spill Count" = "dry_spill_count_yr_pop_density_weighted",
        "Pop-Density Weighted Dry Spill Hours" = "dry_spill_hrs_yr_pop_density_weighted"
      )
    ) +
    tm_layout(legend.text.size = 0.8, legend.title.size = 1) +
    tm_view(set_view = c(-2, 53, 5.5))
}

# Aggregate site-level dry spill data to MSOA polygons and build comparable metrics
# Why: harmonise dry spill intensity by area/population to enable like-for-like comparison
aggregate_dry_spills_to_msoa <- function(data, year) {
  # Convert to sf and join to MSOA
  dry_spill_sites_sf <- data %>%
    filter(year == !!year) %>%
    # Focus on dry_spill variables for the specified condition (2-day, partial data OK)
    select(
      water_company,
      site_id,
      easting,
      northing,
      dry_spill_count_yr = dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak,
      dry_spill_hrs_yr = dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak
    ) %>%
    filter(!is.na(dry_spill_count_yr), !is.na(dry_spill_hrs_yr)) %>%
    # Winsorize raw site-year inputs once to limit extreme leverage upstream
    # Why: apply a single, transparent outlier treatment before any aggregation/derivation
    mutate(
      across(
        c(dry_spill_count_yr, dry_spill_hrs_yr),
        ~ DescTools::Winsorize(
          .x,
          val = stats::quantile(.x, probs = c(0.01, 0.99), na.rm = TRUE)
        )
      )
    ) %>%
    st_as_sf(coords = c("easting", "northing"), crs = 27700)

  sites_with_msoa <- st_join(
    dry_spill_sites_sf,
    msoa_boundaries,
    join = st_within
  )

  # Collapse to MSOA with counts and hours coverage
  msoa_aggregated <- sites_with_msoa %>%
    st_drop_geometry() %>%
    filter(!is.na(msoa_code)) %>%
    group_by(msoa_code, msoa_name) %>%
    summarise(
      dry_spill_count_yr = sum(dry_spill_count_yr, na.rm = TRUE),
      dry_spill_hrs_yr = sum(dry_spill_hrs_yr, na.rm = TRUE),
      site_count = n(),
      water_companies = paste(unique(water_company), collapse = ", "),
      .groups = "drop"
    )

  # Join to boundaries, fill zeros, compute derived metrics with across
  msoa_with_dry_spills <- msoa_boundaries %>%
    left_join(msoa_population, by = "msoa_code") %>%
    left_join(msoa_aggregated, by = c("msoa_code", "msoa_name")) %>%
    mutate(
      dry_spill_count_yr = replace_na(dry_spill_count_yr, 0),
      dry_spill_hrs_yr = replace_na(dry_spill_hrs_yr, 0),
      site_count = replace_na(site_count, 0),
      water_companies = replace_na(water_companies, "None")
    ) %>%
    # Population density: used as context rather than mapped directly
    mutate(
      pop_density = ifelse(
        msoa_area_km2 > 0,
        msoa_population / msoa_area_km2,
        0
      )
    ) %>%
    # Build area- and population-adjusted metrics compactly
    mutate(
      across(
        c(dry_spill_count_yr, dry_spill_hrs_yr),
        ~ .x / msoa_area_km2,
        .names = "{.col}_per_km2"
      ),
      across(
        c(dry_spill_count_yr, dry_spill_hrs_yr),
        ~ ifelse(msoa_population > 0, (.x / msoa_population) * 1000, 0),
        .names = "{.col}_per_1000_pop"
      ),
      across(
        c(dry_spill_count_yr, dry_spill_hrs_yr),
        ~ ifelse(
          msoa_population > 0,
          (.x * msoa_area_km2) / msoa_population,
          0
        ),
        .names = "{.col}_pop_density_weighted"
      )
    ) %>%
    # Log-transform for interpretability under skew; use log1p for zeros
    mutate(
      across(
        c(
          dry_spill_count_yr,
          dry_spill_hrs_yr,
          dry_spill_count_yr_per_km2,
          dry_spill_hrs_yr_per_km2,
          dry_spill_count_yr_per_1000_pop,
          dry_spill_hrs_yr_per_1000_pop,
          dry_spill_count_yr_pop_density_weighted,
          dry_spill_hrs_yr_pop_density_weighted
        ),
        ~ log1p(.x),
        .names = "log_{.col}"
      )
    ) %>%
    # Transform to WGS84 for web mapping
    st_transform(crs = 4326)

  msoa_with_dry_spills
}
```

```{r yearly-dry-plot-spec}
#| eval: false

process_yearly_dry_data <- function(data, year) {
  aggregate_dry_spills_to_msoa(data, year)
}

# Create plots for multiple years with a compact spec table for dry spills
create_yearly_dry_plots <- function(sf_data, year) {
  spec <- tibble::tribble(
    ~key,
    ~var,
    ~title,
    "count_tmap",
    "log_dry_spill_count_yr",
    "Log Total\nDry Spill Count",
    "hrs_tmap",
    "log_dry_spill_hrs_yr",
    "Log Total\nDry Spill Hours",
    "count_popdens_tmap",
    "log_dry_spill_count_yr_pop_density_weighted",
    "Log Dry Spill Count\nPop-Density Weighted",
    "hrs_popdens_tmap",
    "log_dry_spill_hrs_yr_pop_density_weighted",
    "Log Dry Spill Hours\nPop-Density Weighted"
  )

  plots <- purrr::pmap(
    list(spec$var, spec$title),
    ~ plot_dry_spill_map_tmap(sf_data, ..1, ..2)
  )

  rlang::set_names(plots, spec$key)
}
```

```{r yearly-dry-plot-build}
#| eval: false

# Process data and create plots for selected years
years <- c(2023)
dry_plots <- years %>%
  set_names(paste0("dat_dry_sf", str_sub(., 3, 4))) %>%
  map(~ process_yearly_dry_data(dat_yr_clean, .)) %>%
  imap(~ create_yearly_dry_plots(.x, .y))
```

```{r yearly-dry-plot-save}
#| eval: false

# Function to save dry spill tmap plots
save_dry_tmap_plots <- function(plot_list, base_name) {
  tmap_mode("view")

  # Save total plots
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$count_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_dry_total_count.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$hrs_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_dry_total_hours.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )

  # Save population-density weighted plots
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$count_popdens_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_dry_popdens_count.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$hrs_popdens_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_dry_popdens_hours.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )
}

# Save all dry spill plots for each year
walk2(
  dry_plots,
  names(dry_plots),
  ~ save_dry_tmap_plots(.x, .y)
)
```

### Total Dry Spills {#sec-04-04-01-total}

The maps below show the total number of dry spills aggregated to Middle Layer Super Output Areas (MSOAs), a standard statistical geography in England designed to cover areas with broadly similar population sizes (~8000 people or ~3000 households).

MSOAs vary a lot in land area, so raw totals need to be read with care. Even so, relative to the overall spill distribution (see @sec-03-03-01-total), dry spills are concentrated in a smaller set of MSOAs, with especially high activity in the North and South West.

::: panel-tabset
#### Count

```{=html}
<iframe src="book_figs/dat_dry_sf23_dry_total_count.html" width="800" height="600" frameborder="0"></iframe>
```

#### Duration

```{=html}
<iframe src="book_figs/dat_dry_sf23_dry_total_hours.html" width="800" height="600" frameborder="0"></iframe>
```
:::

### Population-Density Weighted Dry Spills {#sec-04-04-02-popdens}

These maps scale dry spills by residents per square kilometre. The North and South West still stand out. On their own, however, the maps do not tell us whether that pattern reflects older sewer infrastructure, higher rainfall, or both.

::: panel-tabset
#### Count

```{=html}
<iframe src="book_figs/dat_dry_sf23_dry_popdens_count.html" width="800" height="600" frameborder="0"></iframe>
```

#### Duration

```{=html}
<iframe src="book_figs/dat_dry_sf23_dry_popdens_hours.html" width="800" height="600" frameborder="0"></iframe>
```
:::

## Frequency and Duration of Dry Spills {#sec-04-07-frequency-duration}

The summary tables and histograms show strong right-skew in both dry-spill frequency and duration. As with other non-negative variables, log transforms make the distribution easier to read.

### Summary Tables {#sec-04-07-01-summary-tables}

```{r build-dry-summary-tables}
# r1_d01_weak: 9km² rainfall, 2-day window, partial data OK
dat_yr_dry_list <- purrr::map(
  2023:2021,
  function(yr) {
    dat_yr %>%
      dplyr::filter(year == yr) %>%
      dplyr::select(
        water_company,
        year,
        dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak,
        dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak
      ) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(
        `Share of sites with any dry spill (count > 0)` = dplyr::if_else(
          dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak > 0, 1L, 0L
        ),
        `Share of sites with annual dry spill duration ≥ 15 minutes (total hours ≥ 0.25)` = dplyr::if_else(
          dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak >= 0.25, 1L, 0L
        )
      ) %>%
      dplyr::rename(
        `Dry Spill Count` = dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak,
        `Dry Spill Duration (hrs)` = dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak,
        `Water Company` = water_company
      )
  }
)
```

::: panel-tabset
#### 2023

```{r show-dry-summary-2023}
modelsummary::datasummary_skim(dat_yr_dry_list[[1]], fmt = 2)
```

#### 2022

```{r show-dry-summary-2022}
modelsummary::datasummary_skim(dat_yr_dry_list[[2]], fmt = 2)
```

#### 2021

```{r show-dry-summary-2021}
modelsummary::datasummary_skim(dat_yr_dry_list[[3]], fmt = 2)
```

:::

### Histogram in 2023 {#sec-04-07-02-histogram}

```{r hist-dry-helpers}
plot_hist_dry <- function(data = dat_yr, yr, x_var, x_label, bins, title) {
  dplyr::filter(data, year == yr) %>%
    dplyr::filter({{ x_var }} > 0) %>%
    (\(df) ggplot(df, aes(x = {{ x_var }})))() +
    geom_histogram(
      bins = bins,
      fill = viridis::viridis(1, option = "magma"),
      alpha = 0.7,
      col = "black"
    ) +
    labs(
      title = title,
      x = x_label,
      y = "Count"
    ) +
    theme_pref
}

plot_hist_norm_dry <- function(
  data = dat_yr,
  yr,
  x_var,
  x_label,
  bins,
  title
) {
  data <- dplyr::filter(data, year == yr) %>%
    dplyr::filter({{ x_var }} > 0) %>%
    dplyr::mutate({{ x_var }} := log(1 + {{ x_var }}))

  mu <- mean(dplyr::pull(data, {{ x_var }}), na.rm = TRUE)
  sigma <- stats::sd(dplyr::pull(data, {{ x_var }}), na.rm = TRUE)

  ggplot(data, aes(x = {{ x_var }})) +
    geom_histogram(
      aes(y = ..density..),
      bins = bins,
      fill = viridis::viridis(1, option = "magma"),
      alpha = 0.6,
      col = "black"
    ) +
    stat_function(
      fun = dnorm,
      args = list(mean = mu, sd = sigma),
      color = viridis::viridis(3, option = "magma")[2],
      alpha = 0.7,
      linewidth = 0.8
    ) +
    labs(
      title = title,
      x = x_label,
      y = "Density"
    ) +
    theme_pref
}
```

::: panel-tabset
#### Dry Spill Duration

```{r hist-dry-hrs-2023}
plot_hist_dry(
  yr = 2023,
  x_var = dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak,
  x_label = "Dry Spill Hours",
  bins = 50,
  title = "Duration of Dry Spills of all Water Companies in 2023 (>0)"
)
```

#### Dry Spill Count

```{r hist-dry-count-2023}
plot_hist_dry(
  yr = 2023,
  x_var = dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak,
  x_label = "Dry Spill Count",
  bins = 60,
  title = "Count of Dry Spills of all Water Companies in 2023 (>0)"
)
```

#### Log Dry Spill Duration

```{r hist-dry-log-hrs-2023}
plot_hist_norm_dry(
  yr = 2023,
  x_var = dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak,
  x_label = "Dry Spill Hours",
  bins = 50,
  title = "(1+Log) Duration of Dry Spills of all Water Companies in 2023 (>0) with Normal Curve"
)
```

#### Log Dry Spill Count

```{r hist-dry-log-count-2023}
plot_hist_norm_dry(
  yr = 2023,
  x_var = dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak,
  x_label = "Dry Spill Count",
  bins = 50,
  title = "(1+Log) Count of Dry Spills of all Water Companies in 2023 (>0) with Normal Curve"
)
```

:::

## Temporal Dry Spill Distribution {#sec-04-08-temporal}

Dry spills are also seasonal. Monthly totals tend to be higher in the wetter months, roughly October to March, though the contrast is smaller than the rainfall patterns alone would suggest.

```{r clean-dry-data-mo}
# Build monthly dry-spill panel (r1_d01_weak)
dat_mo_dry_clean <- dat_mo %>%
  dplyr::filter(dry_spill_hrs_mo_r1_d01_weak >= 0) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    month = (month_id - 1) %% 12 + 1,
    year = (month_id - 1) %/% 12 + 2021,
    month = factor(month, levels = 1:12, labels = month.abb),
    year = factor(year)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(year) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    # Per-year winsorization at 1st/99th percentile for dry counts and hours
    dplyr::across(
      c(dry_spill_count_mo_r1_d01_weak, dry_spill_hrs_mo_r1_d01_weak),
      ~ DescTools::Winsorize(
        .x,
        val = stats::quantile(.x, probs = c(0.01, 0.99), na.rm = TRUE)
      )
    )
  ) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(year, month) %>%
  dplyr::summarize(
    dry_spill_hrs_mo = sum(dry_spill_hrs_mo_r1_d01_weak, na.rm = TRUE),
    dry_spill_count_mo = sum(dry_spill_count_mo_r1_d01_weak, na.rm = TRUE),
    unique_spill_sites = dplyr::n_distinct(site_id),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(
      c(dry_spill_hrs_mo, dry_spill_count_mo),
      ~ log(1 + .),
      .names = "log_{.col}"
    ),
    dplyr::across(
      c(dry_spill_hrs_mo, dry_spill_count_mo),
      ~ . / unique_spill_sites,
      .names = "{.col}_site"
    )
  )
```

```{r clean-rain-data-mo}
# Build monthly rainfall panel (9 km^2: 3×3 grid max)
dat_mo_rain_clean <- rainfall_mo |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    month = factor(month, levels = 1:12, labels = month.abb),
    year = factor(year)
  ) |>
  dplyr::group_by(year, month) |>
  dplyr::summarize(
    avg_rainfall_mo_r9 = mean(rainfall_r9_mo, na.rm = TRUE),
    med_rainfall_mo_r9 = median(rainfall_r9_mo, na.rm = TRUE),
    unique_spill_sites = dplyr::n_distinct(site_id),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
```

### Total Dry Spill Duration and Count {#sec-04-08-01-total}

::: panel-tabset
#### Dry Spill Duration

```{r temporal-dry-hrs-plot}
plot_monthly_bars <- function(data, y_var, title, y_label) {
  ggplot(data, aes(x = month, y = {{ y_var }}, fill = year)) +
    geom_bar(
      stat = "identity",
      position = position_dodge(width = 0.9),
      width = 0.8,
      alpha = 0.9
    ) +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "magma") +
    labs(
      title = title,
      y = y_label,
      x = NULL,
      fill = "Year"
    ) +
    theme_pref +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1)))
}

fig_dry_temporal_hrs <- plot_monthly_bars(
  dat_mo_dry_clean,
  dry_spill_hrs_mo,
  "Monthly Dry Spill Duration by Year",
  "Dry Spill Duration per Month (hrs)"
)

fig_dry_temporal_hrs
```

#### Dry Spill Count

```{r temporal-dry-count-plot}
fig_dry_temporal_count <- plot_monthly_bars(
  dat_mo_dry_clean,
  dry_spill_count_mo,
  "Monthly Dry Spill Count by Year",
  "Dry Spill Count per Month"
)

fig_dry_temporal_count
```

#### Rainfall (9 km^2)

```{r temporal-rain-plot}
fig_rain_temporal <- plot_monthly_bars(
  dat_mo_rain_clean,
  avg_rainfall_mo_r9,
  "Monthly Rainfall (9 km^2) by Year",
  "Average of site-level monthly rainfall totals (mm), 9 km² grid"
)

fig_rain_temporal
```

:::

### Per Unique Dry Spill Site {#sec-04-08-02-unique}

::: panel-tabset
#### Dry Spill Duration

```{r temporal-dry-hrs-plot-standardised}
plot_monthly_bars(
  dat_mo_dry_clean,
  dry_spill_hrs_mo_site,
  "Monthly Dry Spill Duration by Year per Unique Active Spill Site",
  "Dry Spill Duration per Month (hrs)"
)
```

#### Dry Spill Count

```{r temporal-dry-count-plot-standardised}
plot_monthly_bars(
  dat_mo_dry_clean,
  dry_spill_count_mo_site,
  "Monthly Dry Spill Count by Year",
  "Dry Spill Count per Month"
)
```

#### Rainfall (9 km^2)

```{r temporal-rain-plot-standardised}
# Average monthly rainfall per site is already a per-site metric.
plot_monthly_bars(
  dat_mo_rain_clean,
  avg_rainfall_mo_r9,
  "Monthly Rainfall (9 km^2) by Year per Unique Active Spill Site",
  "Average of site-level monthly rainfall totals (mm), 9 km² grid"
)
```

:::

## Dry Spill Distribution by Water Company {#sec-04-09-company}

The violin plots and bar charts below show dry-spill count and duration by water company, normalised by the number of active spill sites. The broad pattern mirrors overall spill activity, with some companies showing both heavier right tails and higher averages.

### Violin Plots {#sec-04-09-01-violin}

```{r build-dry-violin-plots}
# Generate a dry-only plot for each year (r1_d01_weak)
plots_list_dry_violin <- purrr::map(2023:2021, function(yr) {
  # Filter data by year
  data_year <- dat_yr %>%
    dplyr::filter(year == yr)

  # Reshape to long format for both dry metrics
  data_long <- data_year %>%
    tidyr::pivot_longer(
      cols = c(dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak, dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak),
      names_to = "dry_metric",
      values_to = "value"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      dry_metric = dplyr::recode(
        dry_metric,
        dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak = "Dry Spill Count",
        dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak = "Dry Spill Duration"
      )
    )

  # Violin with overlaid boxplots, facets by metric
  ggplot(data_long, aes(x = water_company, y = value, fill = water_company)) +
    geom_violin(trim = FALSE, alpha = 0.7, scale = "width") +
    geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, fill = "white", outlier.shape = NA) +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "magma") +
    facet_wrap(~dry_metric, scales = "free_y") +
    labs(
      title = paste(
        "Distribution of Dry Spill Statistics by Water Company in",
        yr
      ),
      x = "Water Company",
      y = "Value"
    ) +
    theme_pref +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
})
```

::: panel-tabset
#### 2023

```{r show-dry-violin-2023}
plots_list_dry_violin[[1]]
```

#### 2022

```{r show-dry-violin-2022}
plots_list_dry_violin[[2]]
```

#### 2021

```{r show-dry-violin-2021}
plots_list_dry_violin[[3]]
```

:::

### Bar Charts {#sec-04-09-02-bar}

```{r build-dry-bar-plots}
plots_list_dry_bar <- purrr::map(2023:2021, function(yr) {
  # Filter data for the specified year
  data_year <- dat_yr %>%
    dplyr::filter(year == yr)

  # Reshape to long format and calculate average values by water company and metric
  data_summary <- data_year %>%
    tidyr::pivot_longer(
      cols = c(dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak, dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak),
      names_to = "dry_metric",
      values_to = "value"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::group_by(water_company, dry_metric) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(avg_value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      dry_metric = dplyr::recode(
        dry_metric,
        dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak = "Dry Spill Count",
        dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak = "Dry Spill Duration"
      )
    )

  # Bar chart (averages) with uniform viridis magma fill
  ggplot(data_summary, aes(x = water_company, y = avg_value)) +
    geom_bar(
      stat = "identity",
      fill = viridis::viridis(1, option = "magma"),
      alpha = 0.7,
      colour = "black",
      position = "dodge"
    ) +
    facet_wrap(~dry_metric, scales = "free_y") +
    labs(
      title = paste("Average Dry Spill Statistics by Water Company in", yr),
      x = "Water Company",
      y = "Average Value"
    ) +
    theme_pref
})
```

::: panel-tabset
#### 2023

```{r show-dry-bar-2023}
plots_list_dry_bar[[1]]
```

#### 2022

```{r show-dry-bar-2022}
plots_list_dry_bar[[2]]
```

#### 2021

```{r show-dry-bar-2021}
plots_list_dry_bar[[3]]
```

:::

## Dry vs All Spill Correlation {#sec-04-05-dry-all-corr}

The plots below show how closely dry spills track overall spill activity at the site-year level. They are a useful comparison, but they sit after the main dry-spill results because they are descriptive rather than part of the dry-spill definition itself.

::: panel-tabset
#### Spill Count

```{r corr-dry-all-count}
# Prepare data
corr_dat <- dat_yr %>%
  dplyr::select(
    water_company,
    year,
    site_id,
    spill_count_yr,
    dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    water_company = as.factor(water_company),
    year = as.factor(year)
  )

# Correlation (complete cases)
correlation_count <- cor(
  corr_dat$spill_count_yr,
  corr_dat$dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

# Scatter with 1:1 reference
p_count <- ggplot(
  corr_dat,
  aes(
    x = spill_count_yr,
    y = dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak,
    color = water_company,
    shape = year,
    text = paste(
      "Water Company:", water_company,
      "<br>Year:", year,
      "<br>All Spill Count:", spill_count_yr,
      "<br>Dry Spill Count:", dry_spill_count_yr_r1_d01_weak,
      "<br>Site ID:", site_id
    )
  )
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "black", linewidth = 0.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Dry vs All: Spill Count Correlation",
    subtitle = paste0("r = ", round(correlation_count, 3)),
    x = "All Spill Count (yearly)",
    y = "Dry Spill Count (yearly, 9km², 2-day, partial)",
    color = "Water Company",
    shape = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 11),
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

interactive_plot_count <- ggplotly(
  p_count,
  tooltip = "text",
  dynamicTicks = TRUE
) %>%
  plotly::layout(
    showlegend = FALSE,
    title = list(
      text = paste0(
        "Dry vs All: Spill Count Correlation<br>",
        "<sup>r = ", round(correlation_count, 3), "</sup>"
      ),
      x = 0.5,
      font = list(size = 12, family = "Arial")
    )
  ) %>%
  config(displayModeBar = FALSE)

interactive_plot_count
```

#### Spill Duration

```{r corr-dry-all-hrs}
# Prepare data
corr_dat_hrs <- dat_yr %>%
  dplyr::select(
    water_company,
    year,
    site_id,
    spill_hrs_yr,
    dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    water_company = as.factor(water_company),
    year = as.factor(year)
  )

# Correlation (complete cases)
correlation_hrs <- cor(
  corr_dat_hrs$spill_hrs_yr,
  corr_dat_hrs$dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

# Scatter with 1:1 reference
p_hrs <- ggplot(
  corr_dat_hrs,
  aes(
    x = spill_hrs_yr,
    y = dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak,
    color = water_company,
    shape = year,
    text = paste(
      "Water Company:", water_company,
      "<br>Year:", year,
      "<br>All Spill Hours:", spill_hrs_yr,
      "<br>Dry Spill Hours:", dry_spill_hrs_yr_r1_d01_weak,
      "<br>Site ID:", site_id
    )
  )
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "black", linewidth = 0.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Dry vs All: Spill Duration Correlation",
    subtitle = paste0("r = ", round(correlation_hrs, 3)),
    x = "All Spill Duration (hours, yearly)",
    y = "Dry Spill Duration (hours, yearly, 9km², 2-day, partial)",
    color = "Water Company",
    shape = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 11),
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

interactive_plot_hrs <- ggplotly(
  p_hrs,
  tooltip = "text",
  dynamicTicks = TRUE
) %>%
  plotly::layout(
    showlegend = FALSE,
    title = list(
      text = paste0(
        "Dry vs All: Spill Duration Correlation<br>",
        "<sup>r = ", round(correlation_hrs, 3), "</sup>"
      ),
      x = 0.5,
      font = list(size = 12, family = "Arial")
    )
  ) %>%
  config(displayModeBar = FALSE)

interactive_plot_hrs
```
:::
